data_freshness_note: Most data reflects 2021–2022 school year; cyberbullying data extends through 2024. Elementary-specific data is limited; available statistics primarily capture ages 12–18. Texas data (2016–2023) included where available.

categories.name: Physical Bullying
categories.description: At ages 8–10, physical bullying includes hitting, kicking, pushing, shoving, tripping, spitting, or intentionally damaging property. At this age, it is often unplanned reactions to peer conflicts but can become more deliberate with practice.
categories.prevalence_note: Physical bullying decreases with age; at this elementary stage, it occurs more frequently than in middle/high school. Males report experiencing physical bullying at higher rates (6% vs. 3.7% females nationally).
age_specific_behaviors: Pushing or shoving on playground/in hallways
Taking or breaking another child's belongings
Hitting or kicking during play escalation
Spitting or other gross physical contact
Excluding from games or physical activities
warning_signs: Unexplained bruises, scratches, or torn clothing
Reluctance to go to recess or specific locations
Fear of particular peers or situations
Complaints of physical pain (stomach, headaches)
Becoming aggressive or retaliatory toward peers

categories.name: Verbal/Name-Calling Bullying
categories.description: Name-calling, insults, teasing, and mocking. At ages 8–10, children understand the power of words and intentionally use them to hurt. This is the most common form of bullying reported by this age group.
categories.prevalence_note: Verbal bullying is the most frequently reported type across all school grades. At this age, children are developmentally capable of deliberate verbal cruelty but have limited impulse control and empathy.
age_specific_behaviors: Persistent name-calling (fat, stupid, ugly, etc.)
Mocking appearance, interests, or family background
Making jokes at another child's expense
Insulting intelligence or athletic ability
Public humiliation or embarrassment tactics
Teasing that escalates when child shows upset
warning_signs: Child becomes withdrawn or quiet at school
Loss of interest in social activities
Expressions of feeling 'stupid' or 'ugly'
Reluctance to speak up or participate in class
Increased anxiety around peers

categories.name: Social/Relational Bullying
categories.description: Spreading rumors, excluding from peer groups, deliberately leaving out, or turning friends against a target. At ages 8–10, this involves subtle social manipulation. Females report higher rates of rumor-based and exclusion bullying (16.6% rumors vs. 9.7% males; 4.9% exclusion vs. 2.6% males).
categories.prevalence_note: Relational aggression increases significantly at this age as social awareness develops. Girls are more likely to engage in and be targets of relational bullying.
age_specific_behaviors: Spreading false stories or rumors about a child
Deliberately excluding from group activities or friend groups
Organizing peer rejection (turning others against target)
Whispering or giving meaningful looks to make child feel unwelcome
Creating exclusive groups designed to include some and exclude others
Threatening friendship withdrawal ('if you do this, we won't be your friend')
warning_signs: Child reports 'no one likes me' or 'everyone hates me'
Sudden loss of friendships
Avoidance of group activities or lunch
Comments about what others are saying behind their back
Withdrawal and isolation from peer groups
Complaints of stomach aches or school avoidance related to social situations

categories.name: Cyberbullying
categories.description: Bullying through digital means (texts, social media, gaming platforms). At ages 8–10, access varies widely; some have devices/social media, others do not. Includes mean messages, exclusion from online groups, sharing embarrassing content, or spreading rumors online.
categories.prevalence_note: Cyberbullying is still emerging at this age but increasing. Only 6% of elementary schools report weekly cyberbullying incidents (vs. 37% middle, 25% high school). However, among tweens (ages 10–12), 20.9% report cyberbullying exposure.
age_specific_behaviors: Receiving mean or threatening texts/messages
Being excluded from online gaming groups or chats
Having embarrassing photos or videos shared online
Mean comments posted on social media or gaming platforms
Rumors spread through text/social media
Being blocked or unfriended in an organized, public way
warning_signs: Secretive behavior with devices; quickly closing apps or screens
Visible emotional distress after using phone/computer
Withdrawal from devices child previously enjoyed
Anxiety about checking messages or social media
Reluctance to discuss online friendships
Sleep disruption (checking phone at night for messages)

categories.name: Identity-Based Bullying
categories.description: Bullying targeting race, ethnicity, religion, disability, perceived sexual orientation, or other identity characteristics. At this age, children are developing awareness of differences and may use identity as a bullying tool without full understanding of impact.
categories.prevalence_note: Most common targets: appearance (32.7%), race (13%), disability (9.7%), ethnic origin (9.4%), gender (9.2%), sexual orientation (8.9%), religion (5.8%). Students with disabilities experience 24.5% bullying prevalence in elementary school.
age_specific_behaviors: Racial slurs, ethnic stereotypes, or mocking accents
Religious mockery (dietary restrictions, holidays, practices)
Mocking disabilities, speech impediments, or physical differences
Gender-based teasing (too feminine/masculine, clothes, interests)
Exclusion based on race, family background, or body type
Comments about how a child 'looks different' or 'talks funny'
warning_signs: Child reports being targeted for 'being different'
Expressions of shame about cultural or family identity
Avoidance of cultural activities or expression
Questions about why they 'aren't like other kids'
Anxiety or withdrawal around specific groups
Internalized negative self-perception related to identity

who_is_targeted.summary: Children ages 8–10 who are bullied often share characteristics of perceived vulnerability, social isolation, or visible differences. At this age, children with low self-esteem, anxiety, or social difficulties are at heightened risk. Physical characteristics (size, appearance), disabilities, and those with fewer friendships are disproportionately targeted.
risk_factors: Low self-esteem; strong predictor of victimization across grade levels
Social isolation or few close friendships
Students with disabilities (24.5% bullying rate in elementary)
Neurodivergent children (autism, ADHD); autistic students report highest rates among neurodivergent peers
Children perceived as physically smaller or weaker
Poor social skills or difficulty with peer relationships
History of behavioral or externalizing problems in early grades (K-2)
Low academic achievement or school engagement
Children experiencing family distress, maltreatment, or domestic violence
Higher school poverty or attending larger schools
protective_factors: Close, supportive friendships (at least one best friend)
Peer social support; peer acceptance buffers bullying risk
Strong family support and open communication with parents
School connectedness; feeling safe and valued at school
Good impulse control and emotional regulation skills
Involvement in school activities or extracurricular programs
Positive teacher relationships and feeling liked by adults

who_bullies.summary: At ages 8–10, children who bully may do so for various reasons: seeking power/control, low frustration tolerance, modeling behavior witnessed at home, or peer pressure. Unlike older bullies, younger children may not fully understand the harm they cause. Many children who bully also experience bullying themselves (bully-victims).
common_traits: Externalizing behavior problems in K-2 (strong predictor; 0.56 effect size)
Higher levels of aggression or impulsivity
Average to above-average self-esteem (differs from victims)
Difficulty managing emotions; low frustration tolerance
Lack of empathy or difficulty understanding impact on others
Need for control or dominance in relationships
Peer acceptance or high social status (sometimes)
History of harsh parenting, family conflict, or domestic violence exposure
Limited problem-solving or conflict resolution skills
motivations: Seeking power, control, or dominance in peer relationships
Retaliation or response to their own bullying victimization
Peer pressure; maintaining status within a peer group
Low frustration tolerance; using bullying as emotional outlet
Modeling behavior witnessed at home or in media
Lack of empathy; not understanding victim's emotional response
Gaining attention or social reward from peers who laugh/encourage
Access to resources/power (size, popularity, social influence)

bystander_behavior.summary: At ages 8–10, most children witness bullying at school (70.6% report witnessing; 71% say bullying is a problem). However, only about 44% of bullied students report it to an adult—lower reporting rate than older children. Bystanders often feel uncertain about how to intervene safely or fear becoming targets themselves.
bystander_behavior.typical_response: Bystanders at this age typically either passively watch (fear of retaliation, uncertainty about role), laugh or encourage the bully (seeking social acceptance), or try to distract the bullied child. Very few intervene directly. Younger children (especially 6th-graders) are more likely to report bullying to adults (62%) than older students.
bystander_behavior.intervention_rate: Approximately 44% of students overall report bullying to a trusted adult; younger elementary students show higher reporting (6th-graders 62%, 7th-graders 57%). However, willingness to intervene as upstanders (stopping bullying, supporting victim) is estimated at only 10–20% without specific training.

environments: Classroom (39% of bullying occurs here); often during transitions, independent work, or when teacher is occupied
Hallways and stairwells (37.5%); between classes or at passing times
Playground/recess (high rate at this age); with minimal adult supervision
School bus (significant at this age); students without adult monitoring
Locker rooms or bathrooms; isolated, unsupervised spaces
Online spaces (emerging at this age); texts, social media, gaming platforms where children have access
commonalities.gender_patterns: Females report higher overall bullying rates (21.8% vs. 16.7% males). Physical bullying is higher among males (6% vs. 3.7% females). Relational/social bullying and rumor-spreading are significantly higher for females (16.6% rumors vs. 9.7% males; 4.9% exclusion vs. 2.6% males). Males and females differ in bullying style: boys engage more in power-based aggression; girls in affiliation-based (social manipulation). Girls experience higher emotional impacts of bullying.
commonalities.developmental_context: Ages 8–10 represent a critical window. Children are developing theory of mind (understanding others' mental states) but still have limited empathy and impulse control. Social hierarchy and peer status become increasingly important; exclusion from peer groups causes real distress. Children understand that words hurt and reputation matters. However, they lack the maturity to fully foresee long-term consequences of their actions. This is a 'ripe' age for bullying prevention because interventions at this stage are highly effective; bullying patterns established now often persist if unaddressed.

outcomes.stat: Bullying victims: absenteeism and school refusal

outcomes.stat: Bullying victims: sleep disturbances, eating changes

outcomes.value: Persistent negative self-perception and feelings of shame

outcomes.value: Documented in longitudinal studies extending into adulthood

parent_resources.name: StopBullying.gov
parent_resources.description: Comprehensive federal resource from HRSA, CDC, Department of Education. Provides definitions, warning signs, prevention strategies, and state-by-state anti-bullying laws.
parent_resources.best_for: Parents seeking evidence-based information, understanding what bullying is, state-specific legal resources, how to talk to children about bullying

parent_resources.name: PACER Center – National Bullying Prevention Center
parent_resources.description: Advocacy organization with extensive bullying resources, free downloadable guides, Student Action Plan Against Bullying, research statistics, and support for parents whose children have disabilities.
parent_resources.best_for: Parents of children with disabilities, seeking action plans, understanding risk factors for specific populations, access to peer support groups

parent_resources.name: Kids Against Bullying (PACER)
parent_resources.description: Age-appropriate animated resource for elementary school children (grades 3–5 appropriate). Features relatable characters, teaches recognition and response to bullying.
parent_resources.best_for: Teaching children ages 8–10 about bullying, helping them understand their roles, building empathy and inclusion

parent_resources.name: Crisis Text Line
parent_resources.description: 24/7 crisis support via text message. For children experiencing severe distress, suicidal thoughts, or mental health crises related to bullying.
parent_resources.best_for: Immediate crisis intervention; child experiencing severe anxiety, depression, or suicidal ideation

parent_resources.name: National Suicide Prevention Lifeline
parent_resources.description: 24/7 free confidential support for people in suicidal crisis or emotional distress. Can discuss bullying-related mental health concerns.
parent_resources.best_for: Children or parents in mental health crisis; immediate professional support needed

parent_resources.name: American Academy of Pediatrics – HealthyChildren.org
parent_resources.description: Pediatrician-reviewed parenting resource with articles on bullying recognition, mental health impact, and family strategies.
parent_resources.best_for: Parents wanting evidence-based pediatric guidance; understanding developmental context

parent_resources.name: Cyberbullying Research Center
parent_resources.description: Academic research center providing up-to-date information on cyberbullying, prevention strategies, legal resources, and downloadable tip sheets (many in Spanish).
parent_resources.best_for: Parents concerned about online bullying; seeking research-based guidance on digital safety

parent_resources.name: Texas Education Agency – Bullying and Cyberbullying Resources
parent_resources.description: State-specific bullying policy information, David's Law requirements, and school-reporting procedures for Texas families.
parent_resources.best_for: Texas parents; understanding state laws, school responsibilities, David's Law protections

parent_resources.name: Texas School Safety Center – Bullying and the Law
parent_resources.description: Texas-specific legal guidance, bullying checklist for schools, SB 179 (David's Law) information, and scenario-based resources.
parent_resources.best_for: Texas parents seeking understanding of legal protections and school obligations

parent_resources.name: Center for Parent Information and Resources (CPIR) – Bullying
parent_resources.description: Parent-focused hub with links to multiple bullying resources, video guides, and state-specific parent centers.
parent_resources.best_for: Finding local parent advocacy organizations, state-specific resources, comprehensive bullying links

parent_resources.name: National Crime Prevention Council – Bullying Resources
parent_resources.description: Prevention-focused resources for parents, children, and law enforcement regarding bullying and cyberbullying.

parent_resources.name: Mental Health America – Bullying: Tips for Parents
parent_resources.description: Downloadable factsheet on recognizing bullying, understanding mental health impacts, and evidence-based parental responses.
parent_resources.best_for: Parents wanting quick reference guide; mental health impacts of bullying

parent_resources.name: Children's Wisconsin – Parents Act Now! Bullying Prevention
parent_resources.description: Online parent education module covering bullying types, responses, school reporting, and emotional coaching strategies.
parent_resources.best_for: Parents seeking step-by-step guidance; interactive learning about bullying situations

for_parents: Listen calmly and without judgment when your child discloses bullying; praise them for talking to you
Avoid immediate anger or urging retaliation; remain composed to help your child feel safe
Believe your child; validate that bullying is real and not their fault
Ask open-ended questions to understand full context: 'Tell me what happened. Who was involved? When? How many times?'
Document all bullying incidents in writing (dates, times, locations, people involved, what was said/done); keep records for school meetings
Contact the school (principal, teacher, counselor) in writing and request a formal meeting to discuss the bullying
Request that the school conduct an investigation and provide written documentation of findings and response plan
Teach your child three concrete strategies: (1) Tell a trusted adult; (2) Use the 'buddy system'—never be alone; (3) Don't react emotionally when bullied (bullies thrive on reactions)
Practice assertiveness and calm responses with role-play: 'That's not OK' then walk away; practice staying calm even when upset
Help your child identify one or more trusted peers to sit with at lunch, be with at recess, and spend time with after school
Monitor for signs of depression, anxiety, school avoidance; consult school counselor or pediatrician if concerned
Encourage participation in activities where your child feels successful (sports, clubs, arts) to build confidence and friendships
Talk regularly about school, friendships, and emotions; keep lines of communication open
If cyberbullying: keep evidence (screenshots, saved messages); report to platform; consider involving law enforcement if threats present
Work with school to develop a written safety plan specifying what the child will do if bullying occurs again
Check state and school bullying policies; understand your school's reporting and response procedures
If bullying continues or worsens despite school involvement, consider consulting school district administration or state education agency
Seek counseling or therapy for your child if showing signs of depression, anxiety, self-harm, or social withdrawal
conversation_starters: "How was your day? Who did you play with at recess? Any kids being mean today?"
"I noticed you don't want to go to school today. Is something going on? Is anyone being mean to you?"
"Tell me about your friends at school. Do you have someone you sit with at lunch?"
"If someone was being mean to you, how would you want me to help? What would feel safe?"
"That's not your fault. Being bullied is not because of anything you did or said. The person bullying is making a bad choice."
"Let's come up with a plan together. What are three things you could do if this happens again?"
"I'm going to call the school today. We're going to figure this out together."
"You are not alone. A lot of kids deal with bullying. We're going to get help."
"I believe you. I'm proud of you for telling me. That took courage."
"If you see someone else being bullied, it's okay to tell a teacher. That's being a good friend."
"What do you think you'd like the school to do to make this stop?"
"Bullying can happen because someone is having a hard time. That doesn't make it okay, but we can try to understand why."
"Let's practice what you could say. If someone calls you a name, you could say, 'That's not okay,' and then walk away. Want to practice?"
"Who is someone at school you trust? A teacher, counselor, or friend? Can you go to them if this happens again?"
"You deserve to feel safe at school. Let's make that happen."
"I notice you haven't wanted to see your friends as much. Is something going on? Are kids being unkind?"
red_flags_requiring_immediate_action: Child expresses suicidal thoughts or self-harm ideation related to bullying
Physical injuries resulting from bullying; threat of or actual violence
Child refuses to go to school; extreme school avoidance despite parental intervention
Significant changes in mood: sudden withdrawal, depression, severe anxiety, uncontrollable crying
Child expresses hopelessness, worthlessness, or persistent belief they 'can't take it anymore'
Evidence of cyberbullying involving explicit threats, sexual content, or identity-based hate speech
Bullying is escalating despite school reporting and intervention attempts
School staff dismissive or unresponsive to documented bullying complaints
Child showing signs of eating disorder, sleep disturbance, or physical symptoms (headaches, stomach aches) related to stress
Bullying involves criminal behavior (theft, property damage, assault, stalking, threats)
Child is engaging in self-harm (cutting, scratching, picking) related to bullying
Sudden academic decline (failing grades, no homework completion, withdrawal from activities)
Child has acquired weapons, made threats of violence, or expressed intent to hurt self or others
Isolation is severe and complete (no friendships, excluded from all peer groups)
Bullying is happening in collusion with school staff or is being ignored when reported
